metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC5's proving command to verify at least the key structural markers of the ADR (e.g., `grep -q 'impact_model_version' docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md && grep -q 'median(HIGH)' docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md`) so a placeholder file does not pass.
findings.finding: AC5's proving command (`test -f docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md`) only verifies the file exists, not that it contains the recalibration cadence described in the plan body (ship permissive → accumulate → replay → A/B gate → threshold-down proposal → per-version-bump restart). An empty or placeholder file satisfies the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` spec, explicitly state that each row's attrs are read from `row["severity_attributes"]` (not `row` directly), co-locating the fixture schema access protocol with the script specification.
findings.finding: Section D specifies that `ab_impact_model.py` scores the fixture by passing each row as 'an `impact_code` attrs dict', but the fixture's JSON schema nests the attrs one level down under `"severity_attributes"`. The script spec does not co-locate the access protocol (`row["severity_attributes"]` vs `row`), leaving an implementer free to call `impact_code(row)` directly — which yields ~0 for every row (no consequence binaries at the top level), causing the gate to either trivially pass (median separation ≈ 0 < 0.30 → exits non-zero falsely) or produce a misleading result.
findings.location: Section D — `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` spec; contrast with `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` row schema and `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py` line `impact_code(r["severity_attributes"])`
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Section D: 'For each row, pass `row["severity_attributes"]` (not the row dict directly) to `impact_code` — the fixture schema is `{label, note, severity_attributes: {…}}`, matching the shape `test_impact_code.py` already exercises.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 'bump `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` in both sidecars on every formula change' rule to the ADR body (or CLAUDE.md) so implementers know the invariant before merging an impact-model PR.
findings.finding: The `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` bumping convention — 'every new impact-model formula requires bumping the constant in BOTH sidecars before shipping' — is described in the plan but is not explicitly captured in a developer-facing reference (CLAUDE.md or the ADR body). The ADR covers the *cadence* (ship permissive → accumulate → replay → A/B → propose threshold-down), but not the *implementer checklist*. A future developer shipping a silent formula tweak who forgets to bump the constant will pool new-formula and old-formula scores in the same cohort, which is the exact corpus contamination the versioning was designed to prevent.
findings.location: Section B (Impact-model versioning) and Section E (Documented recalibration cadence) / docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md
findings.suggested_fix: In ADR-0036, add a section titled 'Implementer obligations' that states: 'Any change to the scoring formula of `impact_plan` or `impact_code` MUST increment the corresponding `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` constant (`plan-vN` / `code-vN`) in the same PR. Failure to do so silently pools old- and new-formula scores in the calibration replay, invalidating the segmented corpus.' This makes the convention explicit and discoverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (in the cadence ADR or Scope section) who owns bumping `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` when a formula changes, whether both sidecar constants must be bumped atomically, and how version-bump events are recorded.
findings.finding: The lifecycle for bumping `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` when a model formula changes is not defined: who owns the bump, how the two sidecar constants are kept in sync, and whether a version-bump protocol is documented anywhere. Section E mentions 'each `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` change starts a fresh cohort' but provides no ownership or coordination procedure.
findings.location: Section B (versioning) and Section E (cadence ADR)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to the ADR (E) titled 'Version Bump Protocol': 'When either impact formula changes, the implementing developer (a) bumps the relevant `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` constant in the same PR as the formula change, (b) notes the old→new version in the ADR changelog, and (c) verifies the permissive-invariant test still passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in Section C and the corresponding AC) that `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` WARNS or EXITS NON-ZERO when `--impact-model-version` is set but yields zero matching sidecars after filtering.
findings.finding: The `--impact-model-version` filter's behavior when the filtered corpus is EMPTY (no sidecars match the requested version) is unspecified — the script could silently produce an empty/vacuous calibration report that looks like a valid result but contains no data.
findings.location: Section C / AC item 2 ('absent the flag, behaviour is unchanged')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section C: 'If `--impact-model-version` is set and `load()` finds zero matching sidecars, the script prints a clear warning (`WARNING: no sidecars matched version <v>; refusing to produce an empty calibration report`) and exits non-zero.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in Section D (or AC item 4) that `ab_impact_model.py` validates the fixture file exists and is parseable before scoring, and exits non-zero with a clear diagnostic if not.
findings.finding: The A/B script `ab_impact_model.py` does not specify behavior when the fixture file `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` is missing, unreadable, or malformed — the script would fail with an unguided Python traceback rather than a clear diagnostic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` provides any gate coverage NOT already enforced by `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py::test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit`; if not, replace the new script with a thin CLI wrapper that invokes the existing test logic (or just rely on the pinned test) and reserve the script for the cosmetic old-mean transparency print only.
findings.finding: Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` rebuilds scoring logic and a regression-detection gate that already exist as production-pinned tests. `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py::test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` (lines 266–273) already asserts EXACTLY `median(NIT) < 0.30` AND `(median(HIGH) - median(NIT)) > 0.30` against the same `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` fixture using the same `impact_code` function. The helper functions `_load_fixture` and `_median_gap` are already present in that file. The plan's stated rationale for the script — 'regression-detecting gate, EXITS NON-ZERO when not met' — is already provided by pytest's non-zero exit on test failure (which is already in CI). The only unique content the script adds is a cosmetic print of the old `impact` mean 'for transparency', which does not justify a parallel script that duplicates fixture loading, scoring, and gate logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the gate assertions from `ab_impact_model.py` and keep it as a pure reporting/transparency tool (prints both model scores, no sys.exit gate). The regression-detection gate is already owned by `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit`. The 'degenerate scorer FAILS' unit test should live in `test_impact_model_versioning.py` as the plan already specifies — which keeps the new test file as the sole owner of that contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the AC/Tests section whether the code-review blocking invariant in `test_impact_model_versioning.py` should import and call the same assertion already in `test_code_review_ws2.py::test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff` (referencing it) or whether the existing test already satisfies AC line 3 for the code-review gate (so `test_impact_model_versioning.py` only needs the plan-review half).
findings.finding: The permissive-rollout invariant test for code-review (`blocking_enabled` set == `{secret-detection, high-critical-security}`) is already locked by the existing production test `test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff` in `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py`, but the plan neither acknowledges this duplicate nor clarifies whether `test_impact_model_versioning.py` should re-assert the same contract or defer to the existing test.
findings.location: AC line 3 / Section A / Scope 'Tests: tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py (… permissive invariant …)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in Section D that `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` already enforce the ADR-0035 separation contract, and distinguish what `ab_impact_model.py` adds (offline CLI usability, printed old-mean transparency) versus what is already covered by the production test suite.
findings.finding: Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` substantially overlaps with logic already pinned as production regression tests in `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py`: `_median_gap(impact_code)`, `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` (asserts `m_nit < 0.30` AND `m_high - m_nit > 0.30`), and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` all run against the same `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` fixture. The plan does not enumerate these existing tests as consumers/overlap, leaving an executor unaware that the separation contract is already enforced in the test suite.
findings.location: Section D / AC line 4 / Scope 'A/B: docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py (new)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a locking test (analogous to Section A's permissive-invariant guard) that cross-references `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` constants against a pinned expected value or a content-hash of the model function, so any formula change without a version bump fails the suite and forces a deliberate bump.
findings.finding: Section B's IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION versioning invariant is enforced only by convention, with no locking test analogous to the permissive-rollout guard in Section A. A developer who changes the impact_code or impact_plan formula without bumping the module constant silently emits new-formula scores under the old version tag, pooling mixed-formula data in the corpus and defeating the entire segmentation guarantee Sections B and C exist to provide.
findings.location: Section B (Impact-model versioning) and Section A (comparison point: permissive invariant test)
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py`, add a test that imports `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` from both sidecar modules and asserts they equal their expected pinned strings (e.g. `assert plan_sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION == 'plan-v2'`), AND add a separate test that computes `hashlib.sha256(inspect.getsource(impact_code).encode()).hexdigest()[:16]` and asserts it equals a pinned value — so any formula change without a corresponding `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` bump and pinned-hash update fails the suite. This mirrors the `test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation` pattern from `test_threshold_recalibration.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconsider `ab_impact_model.py` as net-new script: the ADR-0035 absolute separation gate (`median(HIGH)-median(NIT)>0.30` AND `median(NIT)<0.30`) is already pinned as a production pytest in `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit`; justify what the standalone script provides beyond the existing CI test, or replace AC4's test requirement with a pointer to the existing test and restrict the new script to the version-segmented live-corpus analysis that the unit tests cannot cover.
findings.finding: Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` repackages logic already pinned as production regression tests. `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py` already contains `_median_gap()`, `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit()` (asserting `m_nit < 0.30` AND `median gap > 0.30`), and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap()` — the identical fixture, scoring function, and absolute-separation gate the plan proposes for the new script. Adding a standalone `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` that duplicates this is an anti-repackaging violation (Rule-of-Three: the prior-art test already exists and already runs in CI).
findings.suggested_fix: Restrict `ab_impact_model.py` to the version-segmented live-sidecar scoring that pytest cannot do (i.e., the corpus-replay path that needs `--impact-model-version`), and remove the fixture-based gate that duplicates `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit`. The AC4 'current fixture PASSES' assertion is already satisfied by the existing production test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact criterion ID strings for the 11-criterion plan-review blocking set (e.g., explicitly list whether 'G1G2' is one ID or two IDs 'G1' and 'G2') so the executor can write the set assertion without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The plan-review blocking set is described as 'COH/E2/E4/F1/G1G2/G5/G6/T1/T4/T5e/T8' and called an '11-criterion set', but 'G1G2' is ambiguous — it could mean one criterion named 'G1G2' (yielding 10 items) or two separate criteria 'G1' and 'G2' (yielding 11). An executor writing the exact-set assertion in the test cannot resolve this without external investigation, risking a silently wrong assertion.
findings.location: Section A, AC line 3 ('PLAN-REVIEW's default_posture == blocking set is EXACTLY the #5-approved 11-criterion set')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'COH/E2/E4/F1/G1G2/G5/G6/T1/T4/T5e/T8' with the explicit Python set literal the test must assert, e.g.: `{"COH", "E2", "E4", "F1", "G1", "G2", "G5", "G6", "T1", "T4", "T5e", "T8"}` (12 items) or `{"COH", "E2", "E4", "F1", "G1G2", "G5", "G6", "T1", "T4", "T5e", "T8"}` (11 items) — whichever matches the actual criterion IDs in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Sections B and C (and the corresponding ACs) whether `impact_model_version` is stored at the event-root level (sibling of the `data` envelope) or inside `build_payload`'s dict (i.e., inside `data`), and align Section C's `data.get(...)` call with whichever level Section B intends.
findings.finding: Section B declares `impact_model_version` a 'NEW top-level key in `build_payload`'s return dict (a scalar sibling of `schema` / `model`)' — implying it lives at the same JSON level as `schema`/`model`. Section C's calibrator reads it as `data.get("impact_model_version")` — implying it lives inside the `data` sub-object of the stored sidecar event. If `schema`/`model` are event-root fields (not nested under `data`), the calibrator's `data.get(...)` would silently always skip every sidecar (key not found), breaking the version-filter join. The plan never clarifies which JSON level the stored event root vs `build_payload` dict correspond to, so an implementer can go in either direction.
findings.location: Section B (sidecar versioning) vs Section C (calibrator replay) vs AC1 and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: If `build_payload` returns the dict that becomes the `data` field of the stored event (the common sidecar pattern), then 'top-level of `build_payload`'s return dict' IS inside `data`, and `data.get("impact_model_version")` is correct — clarify this by replacing 'scalar sibling of `schema` / `model`' with 'top-level key of the payload dict (i.e., accessible as `data["impact_model_version"]` in the stored event)'. If instead the intent is to hoist it to event-root, update Section C to read from the event root and update the AC accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the five existing `build_payload` test consumers (`test_sidecar_enrichment.py`, `test_code_review_artifact.py`, `test_plan_review.py`, `test_code_review_ws4.py`, `test_plan_review_metrics.py`) in the Scope section and confirm that their structural assertions are compatible with the new additive `impact_model_version` key (or note which, if any, require a one-line update).
findings.finding: Five existing test files outside the named scope call `build_payload` on one or both sidecar modules and make structural assertions on the returned dict, but none are enumerated as consumers that need updating when a new top-level `impact_model_version` key is added to the return value.
findings.location: Section B / Scope section — lists only the two sidecar source files; Section 'Tests' names only `test_impact_model_versioning.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in Section B: 'These five existing tests assert on `build_payload` structure; since the key is additive and they use non-exhaustive key checks, no update is required — confirm by running them after the change.' If any test uses an exhaustive key set check, list it explicitly for update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify in Section B why `impact_model_version` is a better fit than bumping the existing `schema` version string (e.g., `plan_review_result_v2`) for segmenting impact-formula cohorts, given that `latest_review_result()` already uses `schema` as a walk-back discriminator and a schema bump would achieve the same corpus segmentation with one key instead of two.
findings.finding: Section B introduces `impact_model_version` as a NEW, parallel top-level sidecar versioning dimension alongside the already-established `schema` key (`plan_review_result_v1` / `code_review_result_v1`), without justifying why the existing schema-version mechanism cannot carry this signal. The `schema` key already acts as a formula-version discriminator: `latest_review_result()` uses `payload.get('schema') == 'plan_review_result_v1'` as its walk-back guard, and bumping the schema string (e.g., `plan_review_result_v2`) would naturally segment old-formula from new-formula sidecars with no new key. The plan does not establish why a second independent version axis is needed.
findings.location: Section B / Scope / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py and src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
